one_line_takeaway: By splitting a harmful request into fragments hidden inside multiple innocuous-looking visual puzzles and reassembling them via a persona-driven, multi-step cross-image reasoning prompt, MIDAS achieves 60-99% jailbreak success (81.46% average ASR across 4 closed-source MLLMs) against strongly aligned commercial MLLMs, substantially beating prior single-image/isolated-cue jailbreak baselines while also evading external safety-classifier defenses.
key_finding: MIDAS substantially outperforms all five baselines across all three benchmarks and all seven models. On HADES, MIDAS reaches ASR/HR of 93.34/4.32 (Gemini-2.5-FT), 94.24/4.21 (QVQ-Max), 97.36/3.65 (Qwen2.5-VL), 72.18/3.12 (GPT-5-Chat) and 61.49/2.48 (GPT-4o), versus the next-best baseline (HIMRD or VisCRA) typically below 45% ASR. Paper abstract-level average across 4 closed-source MLLMs is 81.46% ASR. On MM-SafetyBench(tiny), MIDAS reaches up to 99.16 ASR (Gemini-2.5-FT) vs. VisCRA's 49.70. On the strict AdvBench-50 subset, baselines FigStep and HIMRD collapse to near-0% ASR on GPT-5-Chat, while MIDAS achieves 64.00% ASR (GPT-5-Chat) and >90% on Gemini-2.5-FT (90.00) and QVQ-Max (95.83), with a peak HR of 4.57. Ablation on AdvBench (GPT-4o) shows removing game-style reasoning causes the sharpest ASR drop (80% -> 22%), followed by removing multi-image dispersion (80% -> 50%), removing role-driven induction (80% -> 59%), and removing semantic dispersion while keeping intact semantics (80% -> 70%). MIDAS also drives much longer reasoning traces than VisCRA (avg. 3195.30 tokens vs. 419.64) with harmful keywords appearing later in the response (64.53% normalized position vs. 48.44%), and is markedly more efficient at inference (190.23s vs. HIMRD's 3357.00s on Gemini-2.5-Pro). Against external detectors, LlamaGuard+GPT-4o-mini classified 92% of MIDAS's dispersed inputs as 'safe' despite harmful reconstructed outputs (vs. 8% for the plaintext original), and MIDAS retained higher ASR than VisCRA under both ShieldLM (48.8 vs 51% relative-drop framing) and Self-Reminder (11% relative ASR drop vs. VisCRA's 70% drop) defenses.
core_contribution: MIDAS (Multi-Image Dispersion and Semantic Reconstruction) is a black/gray-box multimodal jailbreak framework for MLLMs. It extracts a small set of risk-bearing tokens (mmax typically 3) from a harmful query, decomposes each into fragments, and disperses those fragments across multiple images (H, default 6) under three constraints: each risk unit must span at least two images (cross-image dispersion), each image carries fragments from only one unit (single-unit isolation), and fragment counts are balanced across images. Each image embeds its fragments inside one of six 'Game-style Visual Reasoning' (GVR) puzzle templates (Letter Equation, Jigsaw Letter, Rank-and-Read, Odd-One-Out, Navigate-and-Read, CAPTCHA) so the fragment is only recoverable by solving an innocuous-looking puzzle. On the text side, the original query is masked into a sanitized placeholder template, wrapped in a hierarchical 'authority-compliance' + 'investigator/strategist' persona (Contextual Binding), and augmented with a persona prompt that reframes the malicious intent as a high-level questioner's plan (Persona-driven Reasoning Induction). At inference, the model must decode each puzzle image, reconstruct the original fragments in order, and then produce a role-consistent harmful plan, all in a single-shot query (no iterative optimization).
threat_model: Black-box or gray-box adversary: no access to model parameters Theta or internal activations, but can query the model and observe outputs; may freely craft/replace input text and images and may use surrogate/offline models to select fragment placements and puzzle templates before querying the target. The attack assumes each individual modality component (a single image, or the sanitized text) is innocuous in isolation and only the fused, reasoned-over representation reveals the harmful intent -- explicitly designed to evade both the target MLLM's internal alignment and external content-filtering pipelines that inspect inputs/outputs in isolation.
limitations: The paper does not report false-positive/collateral costs of the attack (e.g., effect on benign queries), does not evaluate against process-aware or reasoning-trace-monitoring defenses (which the authors themselves suggest as the appropriate countermeasure), and the ablation/hyperparameter studies are run on a single model (GPT-4o) and limited benchmark subset, leaving open how sensitive the k=3, H=6 default is across the full model/benchmark grid. HR is a rated (likely LLM- or human-judged) metric whose scoring protocol details are deferred to an appendix not captured here. The extraction step relies on a 'lightweight extractor prompt' Eta whose reliability/failure modes are not analyzed in the main text.

quantitative_results: MM-SafetyBench(tiny) ASR/HR MIDAS: Gemini-2.5-FT 99.16/4.35, Gemini-2.5-Pro 92.17/3.94, GPT-4o 61.07/2.53, GPT-5-Chat 81.54/3.49, QVQ-Max 98.65/4.21
AdvBench-50 ASR/HR MIDAS: Gemini-2.5-FT 90.00/4.57, Gemini-2.5-Pro 97.96/3.90, GPT-4o 80.00/3.12, GPT-5-Chat 64.00/3.02, QVQ-Max 95.83/4.19
Abstract-level average ASR across 4 closed-source MLLMs: 81.46%
Ablation on AdvBench/GPT-4o (ASR%, HR): Full MIDAS 80, 3.12; w/o Multi-Image 50, 2.04; w/o Dispersion 70, 2.90; w/o Game-Style Reasoning 22, 0.92; w/o Role-Driven Induction 59, 2.53
Runtime (s) on Gemini-2.5-Pro / GPT-5-Chat: HIMRD 3357.00/105.71, VisCRA 258.98/128.47, MIDAS 190.23/55.63
Keyword exposure position / reasoning length (HADES): VisCRA 48.44% / 419.64 tokens vs. MIDAS 64.53% / 3195.30 tokens
LlamaGuard+GPT-4o-mini safe-classification rate: original query 8% safe, HIMRD 90% safe, MIDAS 92% safe
Hyperparameter sweet spot: k=3 keywords, H=6 images (redundancy ratio rho = H/k >= 2)
